    "--local-config-file",
    type=LocalFileInput(exists=True, readable=True, dir_okay=False, resolve_path=True),
    required=False,
    default=None,
    help="A filename containing the dumped configuration values. Internal use only.",
    hidden=True,
    is_eager=True,
)
@click.pass_context
def start(
    ctx,
    quiet=False,
    metadata=None,
    environment=None,
    force_rebuild_environments=False,
    datastore=None,
    datastore_root=None,
    decospecs=None,
    package_suffixes=None,
    pylint=None,
    event_logger=None,
    monitor=None,
    local_config_file=None,
    config=None,
    config_value=None,
    **deco_options
):
    if quiet:
        echo = echo_dev_null
    else:
        echo = echo_always

    ctx.obj.version = metaflow_version.get_version()
    version = ctx.obj.version
    if use_r():
        version = metaflow_r_version()

    echo("Metaflow %s" % version, fg="magenta", bold=True, nl=False)
    echo(" executing *%s*" % ctx.obj.flow.name, fg="magenta", nl=False)
    echo(" for *%s*" % resolve_identity(), fg="magenta")

    # Setup the context
    cli_args._set_top_kwargs(ctx.params)
    ctx.obj.echo = echo
    ctx.obj.echo_always = echo_always
    ctx.obj.is_quiet = quiet
    ctx.obj.logger = logger
    ctx.obj.pylint = pylint
    ctx.obj.check = functools.partial(_check, echo)
    ctx.obj.top_cli = cli
    ctx.obj.package_suffixes = package_suffixes.split(",")

    ctx.obj.datastore_impl = [d for d in DATASTORES if d.TYPE == datastore][0]

    if datastore_root is None:
        datastore_root = ctx.obj.datastore_impl.get_datastore_root_from_config(
            ctx.obj.echo
        )
    if datastore_root is None:
        raise CommandException(
            "Could not find the location of the datastore -- did you correctly set the "
            "METAFLOW_DATASTORE_SYSROOT_%s environment variable?" % datastore.upper()
        )

    ctx.obj.datastore_impl.datastore_root = datastore_root

    FlowDataStore.default_storage_impl = ctx.obj.datastore_impl

    # At this point, we are able to resolve the user-configuration options so we can
    # process all those decorators that the user added that will modify the flow based
    # on those configurations. It is important to do this as early as possible since it
    # actually modifies the flow itself

    # When we process the options, the first one processed will return None and the
    # second one processed will return the actual options. The order of processing
    # depends on what (and in what order) the user specifies on the command line.
    config_options = config or config_value

    if (
        hasattr(ctx, "saved_args")
        and ctx.saved_args
        and ctx.saved_args[0] == "resume"
        and getattr(ctx.obj, "has_config_options", False)
    ):
        # In the case of resume, we actually need to load the configurations
        # from the resumed run to process them. This can be slightly onerous so check
        # if we need to in the first place
        if getattr(ctx.obj, "has_cl_config_options", False):
            raise click.UsageError(
                "Cannot specify --config or --config-value with 'resume'"
            )
        # We now load the config artifacts from the original run id
        run_id = None
        try:
            idx = ctx.saved_args.index("--origin-run-id")
        except ValueError:
            idx = -1
        if idx >= 0:
            run_id = ctx.saved_args[idx + 1]
        else:
            run_id = get_latest_run_id(ctx.obj.echo, ctx.obj.flow.name)
        if run_id is None:
            raise CommandException(
                "A previous run id was not found. Specify --origin-run-id."
            )
        # We get the name of the parameters we need to load from the datastore -- these
        # are accessed using the *variable* name and not necessarily the *parameter* name
        config_var_names = []
        config_param_names = []
        for name, param in ctx.obj.flow._get_parameters():
            if not param.IS_CONFIG_PARAMETER:
                continue
            config_var_names.append(name)
            config_param_names.append(param.name)

        # We just need a task datastore that will be thrown away -- we do this so
        # we don't have to create the logger, monitor, etc.
        debug.userconf_exec("Loading config parameters from run %s" % run_id)
        for d in TaskDataStoreSet(
            FlowDataStore(ctx.obj.flow.name),
            run_id,
            steps=["_parameters"],
            prefetch_data_artifacts=config_var_names,
        ):
            param_ds = d

        # We can now set the the CONFIGS value in the flow properly. This will overwrite
        # anything that may have been passed in by default and we will use exactly what
        # the original flow had. Note that these are accessed through the parameter name
        ctx.obj.flow._flow_state[_FlowState.CONFIGS].clear()
        d = ctx.obj.flow._flow_state[_FlowState.CONFIGS]
        for param_name, var_name in zip(config_param_names, config_var_names):
            val = param_ds[var_name]
            debug.userconf_exec("Loaded config %s as: %s" % (param_name, val))
            d[param_name] = val

    elif getattr(ctx.obj, "delayed_config_exception", None):
        # If we are not doing a resume, any exception we had parsing configs needs to
        # be raised. For resume, since we ignore those options, we ignore the error.
        raise ctx.obj.delayed_config_exception

    new_cls = ctx.obj.flow._process_config_decorators(config_options)
    if new_cls:
        ctx.obj.flow = new_cls(use_cli=False)

    ctx.obj.graph = ctx.obj.flow._graph

    ctx.obj.environment = [
        e for e in ENVIRONMENTS + [MetaflowEnvironment] if e.TYPE == environment
    ][0](ctx.obj.flow)
    # set force rebuild flag for environments that support it.
    ctx.obj.environment._force_rebuild = force_rebuild_environments
    ctx.obj.environment.validate_environment(ctx.obj.logger, datastore)

    ctx.obj.event_logger = LOGGING_SIDECARS[event_logger](
        flow=ctx.obj.flow, env=ctx.obj.environment
    )
    ctx.obj.event_logger.start()
    _system_logger.init_system_logger(ctx.obj.flow.name, ctx.obj.event_logger)

    ctx.obj.monitor = MONITOR_SIDECARS[monitor](
        flow=ctx.obj.flow, env=ctx.obj.environment
    )
    ctx.obj.monitor.start()
    _system_monitor.init_system_monitor(ctx.obj.flow.name, ctx.obj.monitor)

    ctx.obj.metadata = [m for m in METADATA_PROVIDERS if m.TYPE == metadata][0](
        ctx.obj.environment, ctx.obj.flow, ctx.obj.event_logger, ctx.obj.monitor
    )

    ctx.obj.flow_datastore = FlowDataStore(
        ctx.obj.flow.name,
        ctx.obj.environment,
        ctx.obj.metadata,
        ctx.obj.event_logger,
        ctx.obj.monitor,
    )

    ctx.obj.config_options = config_options

    decorators._init(ctx.obj.flow)

    # It is important to initialize flow decorators early as some of the
    # things they provide may be used by some of the objects initialized after.
    decorators._init_flow_decorators(
        ctx.obj.flow,
        ctx.obj.graph,
        ctx.obj.environment,
        ctx.obj.flow_datastore,
        ctx.obj.metadata,
        ctx.obj.logger,
        echo,
        deco_options,
    )

    # In the case of run/resume, we will want to apply the TL decospecs
    # *after* the run decospecs so that they don't take precedence. In other
    # words, for the same decorator, we want `myflow.py run --with foo` to
    # take precedence over any other `foo` decospec

    # Note that top-level decospecs are used primarily with non run/resume
    # options as well as with the airflow/argo/sfn integrations which pass
    # all the decospecs (the ones from top-level but also the ones from the
    # run/resume level) through the tl decospecs.
    ctx.obj.tl_decospecs = list(decospecs or [])

    # initialize current and parameter context for deploy-time parameters
    current._set_env(flow=ctx.obj.flow, is_running=False)
    parameters.set_parameter_context(
        ctx.obj.flow.name,
        ctx.obj.echo,
        ctx.obj.flow_datastore,
        {
            k: ConfigValue(v) if v is not None else None
            for k, v in ctx.obj.flow.__class__._flow_state.get(
                _FlowState.CONFIGS, {}
            ).items()
        },
    )

    if (
        hasattr(ctx, "saved_args")
        and ctx.saved_args
        and ctx.saved_args[0] not in ("run", "resume")
    ):
        # run/resume are special cases because they can add more decorators with --with,
        # so they have to take care of themselves.

        all_decospecs = ctx.obj.tl_decospecs + list(
            ctx.obj.environment.decospecs() or []
        )

        # We add the default decospecs for everything except init and step since in those
        # cases, the decospecs will already have been handled by either a run/resume
        # or a scheduler setting them up in their own way.
        if ctx.saved_args[0] not in ("step", "init"):
            all_decospecs += DEFAULT_DECOSPECS.split()
        if all_decospecs:
            decorators._attach_decorators(ctx.obj.flow, all_decospecs)
            decorators._init(ctx.obj.flow)
            # Regenerate graph if we attached more decorators
            ctx.obj.flow.__class__._init_graph()
            ctx.obj.graph = ctx.obj.flow._graph

        decorators._init_step_decorators(
            ctx.obj.flow,
            ctx.obj.graph,
            ctx.obj.environment,
            ctx.obj.flow_datastore,
            ctx.obj.logger,
        )

        # TODO (savin): Enable lazy instantiation of package
        ctx.obj.package = None

    if ctx.invoked_subcommand is None:
        ctx.invoke(check)


def _check(echo, graph, flow, environment, pylint=True, warnings=False, **kwargs):
    echo("Validating your flow...", fg="magenta", bold=False)
    linter = lint.linter
    # TODO set linter settings
    linter.run_checks(graph, **kwargs)
    echo("The graph looks good!", fg="green", bold=True, indent=True)
    if pylint:
        echo("Running pylint...", fg="magenta", bold=False)
        fname = inspect.getfile(flow.__class__)
        pylint = PyLint(fname)
        if pylint.has_pylint():
            pylint_is_happy, pylint_exception_msg = pylint.run(
                warnings=warnings,
                pylint_config=environment.pylint_config(),
                logger=echo_always,
            )

            if pylint_is_happy:
                echo("Pylint is happy!", fg="green", bold=True, indent=True)
            else:
                echo(
                    "Pylint couldn't analyze your code.\n\tPylint exception: %s"
                    % pylint_exception_msg,
                    fg="red",
                    bold=True,
                    indent=True,
                )
                echo("Skipping Pylint checks.", fg="red", bold=True, indent=True)
        else:
            echo(
                "Pylint not found, so extra checks are disabled.",
                fg="green",
                indent=True,
                bold=False,
            )


def print_metaflow_exception(ex):
    echo_always(ex.headline, indent=True, nl=False, bold=True)
    location = ""
    if ex.source_file is not None:
        location += " in file %s" % ex.source_file
    if ex.line_no is not None:
        location += " on line %d" % ex.line_no
    location += ":"
    echo_always(location, bold=True)
    echo_always(ex.message, indent=True, bold=False, padding_bottom=True)


def print_unknown_exception(ex):
    echo_always("Internal error", indent=True, bold=True)
    echo_always(traceback.format_exc(), highlight=None, highlight_bold=False)


class CliState(object):
    def __init__(self, flow):
        self.flow = flow


def main(flow, args=None, handle_exceptions=True, entrypoint=None):
    # Ignore warning(s) and prevent spamming the end-user.
    # TODO: This serves as a short term workaround for RuntimeWarning(s) thrown
    # in py3.8 related to log buffering (bufsize=1).
    import warnings

    warnings.filterwarnings("ignore")
    if entrypoint is None:
        entrypoint = [sys.executable, sys.argv[0]]

    state = CliState(flow)
    state.entrypoint = entrypoint

    try:
        if args is None:
            start(auto_envvar_prefix="METAFLOW", obj=state)
        else:
            try:
                start(args=args, obj=state, auto_envvar_prefix="METAFLOW")
            except SystemExit as e:
                return e.code
    except MetaflowException as x:
        if handle_exceptions:
            print_metaflow_exception(x)
            sys.exit(1)
        else:
            raise
    except Exception as x:
        if handle_exceptions:
            print_unknown_exception(x)
            sys.exit(1)
        else:
            raise
    finally:
        if hasattr(state, "monitor") and state.monitor is not None:
            state.monitor.terminate()
        if hasattr(state, "event_logger") and state.event_logger is not None:
            state.event_logger.terminate()
tool_calls.result:
### File Overview
`metaflow/cli.py` defines Metaflow’s primary Click-based CLI entrypoint, including the top-level command collection, core built-in commands (e.g., `check`, `show`), and the `start` command that bootstraps runtime context (environment, datastore, metadata, logging/monitoring, decorators/config).

### Architecture
- **Click command structure**
  - `cli` is a `click.group` using `LazyGroup` with several lazily-loaded subcommands (`init`, `dump`, `step`, `run`, `resume`).
  - `start` is the *real* top-level command users execute (decorated with `@click.command(cls=LazyPluginCommandCollection, sources=[cli], lazy_sources=...)`) which **collects**:
    - Built-in commands from `cli`
    - Plugin commands from paths returned by `plugins.get_plugin_cli_path()`
  - `start` is invoked by `main()`, and it sets up `ctx.obj` with all state needed by subcommands and integrations.
- **Context / state propagation**
  - `CliState` holds at least the `flow` instance; `start` enriches `ctx.obj` with many computed/initialized fields (echo/loggers, graph, environment, datastore, metadata, etc.).
  - `cli_args._set_top_kwargs(ctx.params)` records top-level CLI parameters for other parts of the system (notably runner/invocations that need consistent CLI args behavior).
- **Flow validation + tooling**
  - `_check()` runs Metaflow’s linter (`lint.linter.run_checks`) and optional pylint via `PyLint`.
- **Error handling**
  - `main()` wraps execution and prints either structured `MetaflowException` output or an “Internal error” traceback, and ensures monitor/event-logger teardown.

### Functions & Classes
- `echo_dev_null` — No-op echo function used when `--quiet` is enabled; important for suppressing non-critical output uniformly.
- `echo_always` — Central formatted output routine using `click.secho`; supports indentation, padding, and “highlight spans” split by `*...*`. This is the primary human-facing printer used across commands.
- `logger` — Timestamped logging helper for system messages (supports “bad” coloring); stored on `ctx.obj.logger` and used by environment validation and other subsystems.

- `cli` — `click.group(cls=LazyGroup, lazy_subcommands=...)` defining a minimal built-in group whose subcommands are **lazy-imported**. Important because Runner/API exposure depends on how commands are grouped and discovered.

- `check` — `@cli.command`; runs `_check(...)` (Metaflow validations + optional pylint) and prints hints for next commands. Important as the default action when no subcommand is provided (via `start`).
- `show` — `@cli.command`; prints graph documentation and per-step docs/outbound edges.
- `help` — `@cli.command`; prints parent help (`ctx.parent.get_help()`), effectively exposing the full `start` command collection help.
- `output_raw` — `@cli.command`; dumps internal graph representation as string or JSON.
- `output_dot` — `@cli.command`; outputs Graphviz DOT for the flow graph.
- `version` — `@cli.command`; prints resolved Metaflow version (R-aware via `use_r()` in `start`, but this command itself prints `obj.version`).

- `start` — The primary Click command (with `LazyPluginCommandCollection`) that:
  - Combines built-in `cli` commands plus plugin commands (from `plugins.get_plugin_cli_path()`).
  - Defines top-level options that affect nearly all behavior (`--quiet`, `--metadata`, `--environment`, `--datastore`, `--with`, `--package-suffixes`, `--pylint`, `--event-logger`, `--monitor`, plus config-related options via `@config_options`).
  - Initializes `ctx.obj` fields that downstream commands rely on, including:
    - CLI args snapshot: `cli_args._set_top_kwargs(ctx.params)`
    - Output functions: `ctx.obj.echo`, `ctx.obj.echo_always`
    - Version/identity banner
    - Datastore selection and root resolution; sets `FlowDataStore.default_storage_impl`
    - Config handling (including special resume-path loading config artifacts from `_parameters` step using `TaskDataStoreSet`)
    - Decorator processing order and initialization:
      - `decorators._init(...)`
      - `decorators._init_flow_decorators(...)`
      - Conditionally attaches default/top-level decospecs for non-`run`/`resume` commands via `decorators._attach_decorators(...)`, then reinitializes graph
      - `decorators._init_step_decorators(...)` for non-`run`/`resume`
    - Environment instantiation and validation: `MetaflowEnvironment` or plugin environment from `ENVIRONMENTS`
    - Event logger + monitor setup, and system hooks: `_system_logger.init_system_logger`, `_system_monitor.init_system_monitor`
    - Metadata provider instantiation from `METADATA_PROVIDERS`
    - Flow datastore creation: `ctx.obj.flow_datastore = FlowDataStore(...)`
    - Sets up `current._set_env(...)` and `parameters.set_parameter_context(...)` (notably injecting config values via `ConfigValue`)
  - If no subcommand is invoked, it defaults to `ctx.invoke(check)`.

- `_check` — Performs Metaflow graph validations and optional pylint checks; key for “flow is valid” behavior and used by `check` and `ctx.obj.check`.
- `print_metaflow_exception` — Pretty-prints `MetaflowException` fields (`headline`, `source_file`, `line_no`, `message`).
- `print_unknown_exception` — Prints a generic internal error plus full traceback.
- `CliState` — Minimal state container holding `flow`; `main()` adds `entrypoint`.
- `main` — Programmatic entrypoint to execute the CLI for a given `flow`. Handles `args` injection (useful for Runner/API), exception handling (`handle_exceptions`), and termination of monitor/event logger in `finally`.

### Relevance to Agent's Goal
- **Command assembly / exposure for Runner API**
  - The critical structure is that `start` (not `cli`) is the **top-level** Click command constructed as a `LazyPluginCommandCollection` combining:
    - `sources=[cli]` (built-in group with lazy subcommands)
    - `lazy_sources=plugins.get_plugin_cli_path()` (plugin CLI contributions)
  - Runner implementations that introspect available commands/options should focus on `start` as the canonical command tree root, and note `LazyGroup` / lazy subcommand import behavior for `cli`.
- **Metadata relevant to runner click API**
  - `cli_args._set_top_kwargs(ctx.params)` is an important hook for capturing/propagating top-level flags, likely relevant to any “Runner” orchestration that needs to reproduce CLI behavior.
  - `ctx.obj.top_cli = cli` is explicitly stored, which can be used by runner tooling to access the built-in group programmatically.
- **Behavioral nuance that affects runner/invocations**
  - Default behavior when no subcommand is provided: `start` invokes `check`.
  - `resume` has special config-loading behavior (and forbids `--config/--config-value` if configs are present), which can affect runner flows that want parity with CLI semantics.
  - Decorator attachment order differs for `run`/`resume` vs other commands; runner-generated commands may need to mirror this precedence if they construct CLI calls.

from .datastore import FlowDataStore, TaskDataStoreSet

ERASE_TO_EOL = "\033[K"
HIGHLIGHT = "red"
INDENT = " " * 4

LOGGER_TIMESTAMP = "magenta"
LOGGER_COLOR = "green"
LOGGER_BAD_COLOR = "red"


def echo_dev_null(*args, **kwargs):
    pass


def echo_always(line, **kwargs):
    kwargs["err"] = kwargs.get("err", True)
    if kwargs.pop("indent", None):
        line = "\n".join(INDENT + x for x in line.splitlines())
    if "nl" not in kwargs or kwargs["nl"]:
        line += ERASE_TO_EOL
    top = kwargs.pop("padding_top", None)
    bottom = kwargs.pop("padding_bottom", None)
    highlight = kwargs.pop("highlight", HIGHLIGHT)
    if top:
        click.secho(ERASE_TO_EOL, **kwargs)

    hl_bold = kwargs.pop("highlight_bold", True)
    nl = kwargs.pop("nl", True)
    fg = kwargs.pop("fg", None)
    bold = kwargs.pop("bold", False)
    kwargs["nl"] = False
    hl = True
    nobold = kwargs.pop("no_bold", False)
    if nobold:
        click.secho(line, **kwargs)
    else:
        for span in line.split("*"):
            if hl:
                hl = False
                kwargs["fg"] = fg
                kwargs["bold"] = bold
                click.secho(span, **kwargs)
            else:
                hl = True
                kwargs["fg"] = highlight
                kwargs["bold"] = hl_bold
                click.secho(span, **kwargs)
    if nl:
        kwargs["nl"] = True
        click.secho("", **kwargs)
    if bottom:
        click.secho(ERASE_TO_EOL, **kwargs)


def logger(body="", system_msg=False, head="", bad=False, timestamp=True, nl=True):
    if timestamp:
        if timestamp is True:
            dt = datetime.now()
        else:
            dt = timestamp
        tstamp = dt.strftime("%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S.%f")[:-3]
        click.secho(tstamp + " ", fg=LOGGER_TIMESTAMP, nl=False)
    if head:
        click.secho(head, fg=LOGGER_COLOR, nl=False)
    click.secho(body, bold=system_msg, fg=LOGGER_BAD_COLOR if bad else None, nl=nl)


@click.group(
    cls=LazyGroup,
    lazy_subcommands={
        "init": "metaflow.cli_components.init_cmd.init",
        "dump": "metaflow.cli_components.dump_cmd.dump",
        "step": "metaflow.cli_components.step_cmd.step",
        "run": "metaflow.cli_components.run_cmds.run",
        "resume": "metaflow.cli_components.run_cmds.resume",
    },
)
def cli(ctx):
    pass


@cli.command(help="Check that the flow is valid (default).")
@click.option(
    "--warnings/--no-warnings",
    default=False,
    show_default=True,
    help="Show all Pylint warnings, not just errors.",
)
@click.pass_obj
def check(obj, warnings=False):
    if obj.is_quiet:
        echo = echo_dev_null
    else:
        echo = echo_always
    _check(
        echo, obj.graph, obj.flow, obj.environment, pylint=obj.pylint, warnings=warnings
    )
    fname = inspect.getfile(obj.flow.__class__)
    echo(
        "\n*'{cmd} show'* shows a description of this flow.\n"
        "*'{cmd} run'* runs the flow locally.\n"
        "*'{cmd} help'* shows all available commands and options.\n".format(cmd=fname),
        highlight="magenta",
        highlight_bold=False,
    )


@cli.command(help="Show structure of the flow.")
@click.pass_obj
def show(obj):
    echo_always("\n%s" % obj.graph.doc)
    for node_name in obj.graph.sorted_nodes:
        node = obj.graph[node_name]
        echo_always("\nStep *%s*" % node.name, err=False)
        echo_always(node.doc if node.doc else "?", indent=True, err=False)
        if node.type != "end":
            echo_always(
                "*=>* %s" % ", ".join("*%s*" % n for n in node.out_funcs),
                indent=True,
                highlight="magenta",
                highlight_bold=False,
                err=False,
            )
    echo_always("")


@cli.command(help="Show all available commands.")
@click.pass_context
def help(ctx):
    print(ctx.parent.get_help())


@cli.command(help="Output internal state of the flow graph.")
@click.option("--json", is_flag=True, help="Output the flow graph in JSON format.")
@click.pass_obj
def output_raw(obj, json):
    if json:
        import json as _json

        _msg = "Internal representation of the flow in JSON format:"
        _graph_dict, _graph_struct = obj.graph.output_steps()
        _graph = _json.dumps(
            dict(graph=_graph_dict, graph_structure=_graph_struct), indent=4
        )
    else:
        _graph = str(obj.graph)
        _msg = "Internal representation of the flow:"
    echo_always(_msg, fg="magenta", bold=False)
    echo_always(_graph, err=False)


@cli.command(help="Visualize the flow with Graphviz.")
@click.pass_obj
def output_dot(obj):
    echo_always("Visualizing the flow as a GraphViz graph", fg="magenta", bold=False)
    echo_always(
        "Try piping the output to 'dot -Tpng -o graph.png' to produce "
        "an actual image.",
        indent=True,
    )
    echo_always(obj.graph.output_dot(), err=False)


@cli.command(help="Print the Metaflow version")

@click.pass_context
def start(
    ctx,
    quiet=False,
    metadata=None,
    environment=None,
    force_rebuild_environments=False,
    datastore=None,
    datastore_root=None,
    decospecs=None,
    package_suffixes=None,
    pylint=None,
    event_logger=None,
    monitor=None,
    local_config_file=None,
    config=None,
    config_value=None,
    **deco_options
):
    if quiet:
        echo = echo_dev_null
    else:
        echo = echo_always

    ctx.obj.version = metaflow_version.get_version()
    version = ctx.obj.version
    if use_r():
        version = metaflow_r_version()

    echo("Metaflow %s" % version, fg="magenta", bold=True, nl=False)
    echo(" executing *%s*" % ctx.obj.flow.name, fg="magenta", nl=False)
    echo(" for *%s*" % resolve_identity(), fg="magenta")

    # Setup the context
    cli_args._set_top_kwargs(ctx.params)
    ctx.obj.echo = echo
    ctx.obj.echo_always = echo_always
    ctx.obj.is_quiet = quiet
    ctx.obj.logger = logger
    ctx.obj.pylint = pylint
    ctx.obj.check = functools.partial(_check, echo)
    ctx.obj.top_cli = cli
    ctx.obj.package_suffixes = package_suffixes.split(",")

    ctx.obj.datastore_impl = [d for d in DATASTORES if d.TYPE == datastore][0]

    if datastore_root is None:
        datastore_root = ctx.obj.datastore_impl.get_datastore_root_from_config(
            ctx.obj.echo
        )
    if datastore_root is None:
        raise CommandException(
            "Could not find the location of the datastore -- did you correctly set the "
            "METAFLOW_DATASTORE_SYSROOT_%s environment variable?" % datastore.upper()
        )

    ctx.obj.datastore_impl.datastore_root = datastore_root

    FlowDataStore.default_storage_impl = ctx.obj.datastore_impl

    # At this point, we are able to resolve the user-configuration options so we can
    # process all those decorators that the user added that will modify the flow based
    # on those configurations. It is important to do this as early as possible since it
    # actually modifies the flow itself

    # When we process the options, the first one processed will return None and the
    # second one processed will return the actual options. The order of processing
    # depends on what (and in what order) the user specifies on the command line.
    config_options = config or config_value

    if (
        hasattr(ctx, "saved_args")
        and ctx.saved_args
        and ctx.saved_args[0] == "resume"
        and getattr(ctx.obj, "has_config_options", False)
    ):
        # In the case of resume, we actually need to load the configurations
        # from the resumed run to process them. This can be slightly onerous so check
        # if we need to in the first place
        if getattr(ctx.obj, "has_cl_config_options", False):
            raise click.UsageError(
                "Cannot specify --config or --config-value with 'resume'"
            )
        # We now load the config artifacts from the original run id
        run_id = None
        try:
            idx = ctx.saved_args.index("--origin-run-id")
        except ValueError:
            idx = -1
        if idx >= 0:
            run_id = ctx.saved_args[idx + 1]
        else:
            run_id = get_latest_run_id(ctx.obj.echo, ctx.obj.flow.name)
        if run_id is None:
            raise CommandException(
                "A previous run id was not found. Specify --origin-run-id."
            )
        # We get the name of the parameters we need to load from the datastore -- these
        # are accessed using the *variable* name and not necessarily the *parameter* name
        config_var_names = []
        config_param_names = []
        for name, param in ctx.obj.flow._get_parameters():
            if not param.IS_CONFIG_PARAMETER:
                continue
            config_var_names.append(name)
            config_param_names.append(param.name)

        # We just need a task datastore that will be thrown away -- we do this so
        # we don't have to create the logger, monitor, etc.
        debug.userconf_exec("Loading config parameters from run %s" % run_id)
        for d in TaskDataStoreSet(
            FlowDataStore(ctx.obj.flow.name),
            run_id,
            steps=["_parameters"],
            prefetch_data_artifacts=config_var_names,
        ):
            param_ds = d

        # We can now set the the CONFIGS value in the flow properly. This will overwrite
        # anything that may have been passed in by default and we will use exactly what
        # the original flow had. Note that these are accessed through the parameter name
        ctx.obj.flow._flow_state[_FlowState.CONFIGS].clear()
        d = ctx.obj.flow._flow_state[_FlowState.CONFIGS]
        for param_name, var_name in zip(config_param_names, config_var_names):
            val = param_ds[var_name]
            debug.userconf_exec("Loaded config %s as: %s" % (param_name, val))
            d[param_name] = val

    elif getattr(ctx.obj, "delayed_config_exception", None):
        # If we are not doing a resume, any exception we had parsing configs needs to
        # be raised. For resume, since we ignore those options, we ignore the error.
        raise ctx.obj.delayed_config_exception

    new_cls = ctx.obj.flow._process_config_decorators(config_options)
    if new_cls:
        ctx.obj.flow = new_cls(use_cli=False)

    ctx.obj.graph = ctx.obj.flow._graph

    ctx.obj.environment = [
        e for e in ENVIRONMENTS + [MetaflowEnvironment] if e.TYPE == environment
    ][0](ctx.obj.flow)
    # set force rebuild flag for environments that support it.
    ctx.obj.environment._force_rebuild = force_rebuild_environments
    ctx.obj.environment.validate_environment(ctx.obj.logger, datastore)

    ctx.obj.event_logger = LOGGING_SIDECARS[event_logger](
        flow=ctx.obj.flow, env=ctx.obj.environment
    )
    ctx.obj.event_logger.start()
    _system_logger.init_system_logger(ctx.obj.flow.name, ctx.obj.event_logger)

    ctx.obj.monitor = MONITOR_SIDECARS[monitor](
        flow=ctx.obj.flow, env=ctx.obj.environment
    )
    ctx.obj.monitor.start()
    _system_monitor.init_system_monitor(ctx.obj.flow.name, ctx.obj.monitor)

    ctx.obj.metadata = [m for m in METADATA_PROVIDERS if m.TYPE == metadata][0](
        ctx.obj.environment, ctx.obj.flow, ctx.obj.event_logger, ctx.obj.monitor
    )

    ctx.obj.flow_datastore = FlowDataStore(
        ctx.obj.flow.name,
        ctx.obj.environment,
        ctx.obj.metadata,
        ctx.obj.event_logger,
        ctx.obj.monitor,
    )

    ctx.obj.config_options = config_options

    decorators._init(ctx.obj.flow)

    # It is important to initialize flow decorators early as some of the
    # things they provide may be used by some of the objects initialized after.
    decorators._init_flow_decorators(
        ctx.obj.flow,
        ctx.obj.graph,
        ctx.obj.environment,
        ctx.obj.flow_datastore,
        ctx.obj.metadata,
        ctx.obj.logger,
        echo,
        deco_options,
    )

    # In the case of run/resume, we will want to apply the TL decospecs
    # *after* the run decospecs so that they don't take precedence. In other
    # words, for the same decorator, we want `myflow.py run --with foo` to
    # take precedence over any other `foo` decospec

    # Note that top-level decospecs are used primarily with non run/resume
    # options as well as with the airflow/argo/sfn integrations which pass
    # all the decospecs (the ones from top-level but also the ones from the
    # run/resume level) through the tl decospecs.
    ctx.obj.tl_decospecs = list(decospecs or [])

    # initialize current and parameter context for deploy-time parameters
    current._set_env(flow=ctx.obj.flow, is_running=False)
    parameters.set_parameter_context(
        ctx.obj.flow.name,
        ctx.obj.echo,
        ctx.obj.flow_datastore,
        {
            k: ConfigValue(v) if v is not None else None
            for k, v in ctx.obj.flow.__class__._flow_state.get(
                _FlowState.CONFIGS, {}
            ).items()
        },
    )

    if (
        hasattr(ctx, "saved_args")
        and ctx.saved_args
        and ctx.saved_args[0] not in ("run", "resume")
    ):
        # run/resume are special cases because they can add more decorators with --with,
        # so they have to take care of themselves.

        all_decospecs = ctx.obj.tl_decospecs + list(
            ctx.obj.environment.decospecs() or []
        )

        # We add the default decospecs for everything except init and step since in those
        # cases, the decospecs will already have been handled by either a run/resume
        # or a scheduler setting them up in their own way.
        if ctx.saved_args[0] not in ("step", "init"):
            all_decospecs += DEFAULT_DECOSPECS.split()
        if all_decospecs:
            decorators._attach_decorators(ctx.obj.flow, all_decospecs)
            decorators._init(ctx.obj.flow)
            # Regenerate graph if we attached more decorators
            ctx.obj.flow.__class__._init_graph()
            ctx.obj.graph = ctx.obj.flow._graph

        decorators._init_step_decorators(
            ctx.obj.flow,
            ctx.obj.graph,
            ctx.obj.environment,
            ctx.obj.flow_datastore,
            ctx.obj.logger,
        )

        # TODO (savin): Enable lazy instantiation of package
        ctx.obj.package = None

    if ctx.invoked_subcommand is None:
        ctx.invoke(check)


def _check(echo, graph, flow, environment, pylint=True, warnings=False, **kwargs):
    echo("Validating your flow...", fg="magenta", bold=False)
    linter = lint.linter
    # TODO set linter settings
    linter.run_checks(graph, **kwargs)
    echo("The graph looks good!", fg="green", bold=True, indent=True)
    if pylint:
        echo("Running pylint...", fg="magenta", bold=False)
        fname = inspect.getfile(flow.__class__)
        pylint = PyLint(fname)
        if pylint.has_pylint():
            pylint_is_happy, pylint_exception_msg = pylint.run(
                warnings=warnings,
                pylint_config=environment.pylint_config(),
                logger=echo_always,
            )

            if pylint_is_happy:
                echo("Pylint is happy!", fg="green", bold=True, indent=True)
            else:
                echo(
                    "Pylint couldn't analyze your code.\n\tPylint exception: %s"
                    % pylint_exception_msg,
                    fg="red",
                    bold=True,
                    indent=True,
                )
                echo("Skipping Pylint checks.", fg="red", bold=True, indent=True)
        else:
            echo(
                "Pylint not found, so extra checks are disabled.",
                fg="green",
                indent=True,
                bold=False,
            )


def print_metaflow_exception(ex):
    echo_always(ex.headline, indent=True, nl=False, bold=True)
    location = ""
    if ex.source_file is not None:
        location += " in file %s" % ex.source_file
    if ex.line_no is not None:
        location += " on line %d" % ex.line_no
    location += ":"
    echo_always(location, bold=True)
    echo_always(ex.message, indent=True, bold=False, padding_bottom=True)


def print_unknown_exception(ex):
    echo_always("Internal error", indent=True, bold=True)
    echo_always(traceback.format_exc(), highlight=None, highlight_bold=False)


class CliState(object):
    def __init__(self, flow):
        self.flow = flow


def main(flow, args=None, handle_exceptions=True, entrypoint=None):
    # Ignore warning(s) and prevent spamming the end-user.
    # TODO: This serves as a short term workaround for RuntimeWarning(s) thrown
    # in py3.8 related to log buffering (bufsize=1).
    import warnings

    warnings.filterwarnings("ignore")
    if entrypoint is None:
        entrypoint = [sys.executable, sys.argv[0]]

    state = CliState(flow)
    state.entrypoint = entrypoint

    try:
        if args is None:
            start(auto_envvar_prefix="METAFLOW", obj=state)
        else:
            try:
                start(args=args, obj=state, auto_envvar_prefix="METAFLOW")
            except SystemExit as e:
                return e.code
    except MetaflowException as x:
        if handle_exceptions:
            print_metaflow_exception(x)
            sys.exit(1)
        else:
            raise
    except Exception as x:
        if handle_exceptions:
            print_unknown_exception(x)
            sys.exit(1)
        else:
            raise
    finally:
        if hasattr(state, "monitor") and state.monitor is not None:
            state.monitor.terminate()
        if hasattr(state, "event_logger") and state.event_logger is not None:
            state.event_logger.terminate()
summarization.summary:
### File Overview
`metaflow/cli.py` defines Metaflow’s primary Click-based CLI entrypoint, including the top-level command collection, core built-in commands (e.g., `check`, `show`), and the `start` command that bootstraps runtime context (environment, datastore, metadata, logging/monitoring, decorators/config).

### Architecture
- **Click command structure**
  - `cli` is a `click.group` using `LazyGroup` with several lazily-loaded subcommands (`init`, `dump`, `step`, `run`, `resume`).
  - `start` is the *real* top-level command users execute (decorated with `@click.command(cls=LazyPluginCommandCollection, sources=[cli], lazy_sources=...)`) which **collects**:
    - Built-in commands from `cli`
    - Plugin commands from paths returned by `plugins.get_plugin_cli_path()`
  - `start` is invoked by `main()`, and it sets up `ctx.obj` with all state needed by subcommands and integrations.
- **Context / state propagation**
  - `CliState` holds at least the `flow` instance; `start` enriches `ctx.obj` with many computed/initialized fields (echo/loggers, graph, environment, datastore, metadata, etc.).
  - `cli_args._set_top_kwargs(ctx.params)` records top-level CLI parameters for other parts of the system (notably runner/invocations that need consistent CLI args behavior).
- **Flow validation + tooling**
  - `_check()` runs Metaflow’s linter (`lint.linter.run_checks`) and optional pylint via `PyLint`.
- **Error handling**
  - `main()` wraps execution and prints either structured `MetaflowException` output or an “Internal error” traceback, and ensures monitor/event-logger teardown.

### Functions & Classes
- `echo_dev_null` — No-op echo function used when `--quiet` is enabled; important for suppressing non-critical output uniformly.
- `echo_always` — Central formatted output routine using `click.secho`; supports indentation, padding, and “highlight spans” split by `*...*`. This is the primary human-facing printer used across commands.
- `logger` — Timestamped logging helper for system messages (supports “bad” coloring); stored on `ctx.obj.logger` and used by environment validation and other subsystems.

- `cli` — `click.group(cls=LazyGroup, lazy_subcommands=...)` defining a minimal built-in group whose subcommands are **lazy-imported**. Important because Runner/API exposure depends on how commands are grouped and discovered.

- `check` — `@cli.command`; runs `_check(...)` (Metaflow validations + optional pylint) and prints hints for next commands. Important as the default action when no subcommand is provided (via `start`).
- `show` — `@cli.command`; prints graph documentation and per-step docs/outbound edges.
- `help` — `@cli.command`; prints parent help (`ctx.parent.get_help()`), effectively exposing the full `start` command collection help.
- `output_raw` — `@cli.command`; dumps internal graph representation as string or JSON.
- `output_dot` — `@cli.command`; outputs Graphviz DOT for the flow graph.
- `version` — `@cli.command`; prints resolved Metaflow version (R-aware via `use_r()` in `start`, but this command itself prints `obj.version`).

- `start` — The primary Click command (with `LazyPluginCommandCollection`) that:
  - Combines built-in `cli` commands plus plugin commands (from `plugins.get_plugin_cli_path()`).
  - Defines top-level options that affect nearly all behavior (`--quiet`, `--metadata`, `--environment`, `--datastore`, `--with`, `--package-suffixes`, `--pylint`, `--event-logger`, `--monitor`, plus config-related options via `@config_options`).
  - Initializes `ctx.obj` fields that downstream commands rely on, including:
    - CLI args snapshot: `cli_args._set_top_kwargs(ctx.params)`
    - Output functions: `ctx.obj.echo`, `ctx.obj.echo_always`
    - Version/identity banner
    - Datastore selection and root resolution; sets `FlowDataStore.default_storage_impl`
    - Config handling (including special resume-path loading config artifacts from `_parameters` step using `TaskDataStoreSet`)
    - Decorator processing order and initialization:
      - `decorators._init(...)`
      - `decorators._init_flow_decorators(...)`
      - Conditionally attaches default/top-level decospecs for non-`run`/`resume` commands via `decorators._attach_decorators(...)`, then reinitializes graph
      - `decorators._init_step_decorators(...)` for non-`run`/`resume`
    - Environment instantiation and validation: `MetaflowEnvironment` or plugin environment from `ENVIRONMENTS`
    - Event logger + monitor setup, and system hooks: `_system_logger.init_system_logger`, `_system_monitor.init_system_monitor`
    - Metadata provider instantiation from `METADATA_PROVIDERS`
    - Flow datastore creation: `ctx.obj.flow_datastore = FlowDataStore(...)`
    - Sets up `current._set_env(...)` and `parameters.set_parameter_context(...)` (notably injecting config values via `ConfigValue`)
  - If no subcommand is invoked, it defaults to `ctx.invoke(check)`.

- `_check` — Performs Metaflow graph validations and optional pylint checks; key for “flow is valid” behavior and used by `check` and `ctx.obj.check`.
- `print_metaflow_exception` — Pretty-prints `MetaflowException` fields (`headline`, `source_file`, `line_no`, `message`).
- `print_unknown_exception` — Prints a generic internal error plus full traceback.
- `CliState` — Minimal state container holding `flow`; `main()` adds `entrypoint`.
- `main` — Programmatic entrypoint to execute the CLI for a given `flow`. Handles `args` injection (useful for Runner/API), exception handling (`handle_exceptions`), and termination of monitor/event logger in `finally`.

### Relevance to Agent's Goal
- **Command assembly / exposure for Runner API**
  - The critical structure is that `start` (not `cli`) is the **top-level** Click command constructed as a `LazyPluginCommandCollection` combining:
    - `sources=[cli]` (built-in group with lazy subcommands)
    - `lazy_sources=plugins.get_plugin_cli_path()` (plugin CLI contributions)
  - Runner implementations that introspect available commands/options should focus on `start` as the canonical command tree root, and note `LazyGroup` / lazy subcommand import behavior for `cli`.
- **Metadata relevant to runner click API**
  - `cli_args._set_top_kwargs(ctx.params)` is an important hook for capturing/propagating top-level flags, likely relevant to any “Runner” orchestration that needs to reproduce CLI behavior.
  - `ctx.obj.top_cli = cli` is explicitly stored, which can be used by runner tooling to access the built-in group programmatically.
- **Behavioral nuance that affects runner/invocations**
  - Default behavior when no subcommand is provided: `start` invokes `check`.
  - `resume` has special config-loading behavior (and forbids `--config/--config-value` if configs are present), which can affect runner flows that want parity with CLI semantics.
  - Decorator attachment order differs for `run`/`resume` vs other commands; runner-generated commands may need to mirror this precedence if they construct CLI calls.

tool_calls.result:
### File Overview
`metaflow/runner/click_api.py` builds a programmatic API layer on top of Metaflow’s Click-based CLI: it discovers Click commands/options and exposes them as dynamically generated Python methods that produce an argv-style command list.

### Architecture
- **Type mapping + signature synthesis:** A `click_to_python_types` mapping plus `get_annotation()` / `get_inspect_param_obj()` are used to derive Python type hints and `inspect.Signature` objects from Click parameters.
- **Dynamic API object model:** The `MetaflowAPI` base class stores a chained representation of invoked commands (`self._chain`) and can `execute()` it into CLI tokens.
- **Command/group extraction:** `extract_group()` and `extract_command()` turn `click.Group`/`click.Command` objects into callable methods with proper docstrings, signatures, defaults, and annotations.
- **Lazy loading for CLI collections:** `_lazy_load_command()` resolves subcommands on first access via `__getattr__`, binding them onto the instance for subsequent calls.
- **Flow discovery + flow-context-dependent parameter computation:** `extract_flow_class_from_file()` loads the target FlowSpec class, and `MetaflowAPI._compute_flow_parameters()` computes flow parameters (including config processing) used to inject flow params into commands that need them.

### Functions & Classes
- `click_to_python_types` — Maps Click parameter type classes to Python types (including custom Metaflow config/JSON types). This is foundational for generating method annotations and validating kwargs.

- `_method_sanity_check(possible_arg_params, possible_opt_params, annotations, defaults, **kwargs) -> Dict[str, Any]` — Validates provided keyword arguments against discovered Click params:
  - Rejects unknown kwargs.
  - Enforces runtime type checking via `check_type(...)` using generated `annotations`.
  - Normalizes special values into Click-compatible forms (notably `JSON` via `json.dumps`, and `config_value` tuples by converting `ConfigValue`/dict to JSON strings).
  - Builds a normalized structure: `{"args": {...}, "options": {...}, "defaults": defaults}` keyed by CLI option/arg names (without leading dashes).
  - Ensures required params are present.
  This is the core mapping/validation layer between Python calls and CLI invocation.

- `_lazy_load_command(cli_collection, flow_parameters, _self, name)` — Implements lazy subcommand binding:
  - Resolves `flow_parameters` if passed as a string method name (used with `"_compute_flow_parameters"`).
  - Calls `cli_collection.get_command(None, name)` and wraps it with `extract_group(...)` or `extract_command(...)`.
  - Caches the resulting callable on `_self` via `setattr`.
  Critical for CLI command discovery without eagerly traversing everything, and important for correctness when flow context/config affects available parameters.

- `get_annotation(param: click.Parameter) -> TTuple[Type, bool]` — Converts a Click parameter into a Python typing annotation and a `is_vararg` flag:
  - Handles `nargs == -1` (varargs), `required`, `multiple`, and tuple-like nargs.
  This is key to generating usable stubs/signatures for the programmatic API.

- `get_inspect_param_obj(p, kind)` — Builds an `inspect.Parameter` for a Click `Argument` or `Option` plus the corresponding annotation:
  - If vararg, creates a `VAR_POSITIONAL` parameter named `"args"`.
  - Otherwise uses `p.name`, `p.default`, and the derived annotation.
  Central to producing accurate Python-callable method signatures.

- `loaded_modules` — Module-level cache keyed by `flow_file` to avoid re-importing flow modules repeatedly during API creation.

- `extract_flow_class_from_file(flow_file: str) -> FlowSpec` — Loads a Python file as a module and finds exactly one `FlowSpec` subclass:
  - Temporarily prepends the flow’s directory to `sys.path`.
  - Uses `importlib.util.spec_from_file_location` + `exec_module`.
  - Supports `CustomFlowDecorator` by unwrapping via `kls._flow_cls`.
  - Errors if zero or multiple FlowSpec classes found.
  This enables `MetaflowAPI.from_cli(...)` to compute parameters and wrap commands in the correct `flow_context`.

- `class MetaflowAPI(object)` — Base class for all dynamically generated API “nodes” (root, groups, commands):
  - `__init__(self, parent=None, flow_cls=None, config_input=None, **kwargs)` — Stores parent chain, initial command kwargs under `self._API_NAME`, flow class/config input, and a cache for computed parameters.
  - `parent` (property) — Returns `_parent` (or `None`).
  - `chain` (property) — Returns `_chain`, the internal structured representation of invoked commands and parameters.
  - `name` (property) — Returns `_API_NAME` (used as the command name in chain construction).
  - `from_cli(cls, flow_file: str, cli_collection: Callable) -> Callable` — Main entry point to create a programmatic API factory for a given flow file and Click CLI collection:
    - Calls `extract_flow_class_from_file`.
    - Wraps CLI with `config_options_with_config_input(...)` and `add_decorator_options(...)` inside `flow_context`.
    - Creates a dynamic subclass via `type(...)` with:
      - `_API_NAME = flow_file`
      - `_internal_getattr` wired to `_lazy_load_command(..., "_compute_flow_parameters")`
      - `__getattr__` wrapper that re-enters `flow_context` before resolving lazy commands
    - Extracts top-level params via `extract_all_params(cli_collection)` and returns a callable `_method` whose signature/annotations/defaults mirror Click’s parameters and whose body returns the root API object with validated params.
    This is the heart of “expose CLI as Runner methods” behavior.
  - `execute(self) -> List[str]` — Flattens the chain into CLI argv tokens:
    - Walks parents to build the full chain.
    - Appends command names, positional args, and `--option` pairs.
    - Special-cases boolean flags using sentinel `"flag"` to omit values.
    This is where the chain becomes an actual command list suitable for subprocess execution.
  - `_compute_flow_parameters(self)` — Computes flow-level `Parameter` objects (used to inject `--<param>` options into certain commands):
    - Only valid on the root “start” API object.
    - If `CLICK_API_PROCESS_CONFIG` is enabled, “fakes” enough Click processing to resolve config inputs (`config`/`config-value`) including `datastore`/`quiet`, and calls `self._config_input.process_configs(...)`.
    - Calls `self._flow_cls._process_config_decorators(...)` which may return a new flow class, then lists parameters via `self._flow_cls._get_parameters()` excluding `IS_CONFIG_PARAMETER`.
    - Caches results in `_cached_computed_parameters`.
    This is crucial for correct command signatures/option injection when configs can add/modify flow parameters.

- `extract_all_params(cmd_obj: Union[click.Command, click.Group])` — Collects Click params and produces:
  - `params_sigs`: ordered `inspect.Parameter` objects for method signature
  - `arg_parameters` / `opt_parameters`: ordered click params for later validation/mapping
  - `annotations`: typing annotations for each param name
  - `defaults`: default values
  Used by `from_cli()`, `extract_group()`, and `extract_command()` to generate “stub-like” methods.

- `extract_group(cmd_obj: click.Group, flow_parameters: List[Parameter]) -> Callable` — Converts a Click group into a callable method that returns a new dynamic `MetaflowAPI` subclass instance representing that group:
  - Recursively installs subcommands as attributes using `extract_group(...)` / `extract_command(...)`.

### File Overview
`metaflow/runner/click_api.py` builds a programmatic API layer on top of Metaflow’s Click-based CLI: it discovers Click commands/options and exposes them as dynamically generated Python methods that produce an argv-style command list.

### Architecture
- **Type mapping + signature synthesis:** A `click_to_python_types` mapping plus `get_annotation()` / `get_inspect_param_obj()` are used to derive Python type hints and `inspect.Signature` objects from Click parameters.
- **Dynamic API object model:** The `MetaflowAPI` base class stores a chained representation of invoked commands (`self._chain`) and can `execute()` it into CLI tokens.
- **Command/group extraction:** `extract_group()` and `extract_command()` turn `click.Group`/`click.Command` objects into callable methods with proper docstrings, signatures, defaults, and annotations.
- **Lazy loading for CLI collections:** `_lazy_load_command()` resolves subcommands on first access via `__getattr__`, binding them onto the instance for subsequent calls.
- **Flow discovery + flow-context-dependent parameter computation:** `extract_flow_class_from_file()` loads the target FlowSpec class, and `MetaflowAPI._compute_flow_parameters()` computes flow parameters (including config processing) used to inject flow params into commands that need them.

### Functions & Classes
- `click_to_python_types` — Maps Click parameter type classes to Python types (including custom Metaflow config/JSON types). This is foundational for generating method annotations and validating kwargs.

- `_method_sanity_check(possible_arg_params, possible_opt_params, annotations, defaults, **kwargs) -> Dict[str, Any]` — Validates provided keyword arguments against discovered Click params:
  - Rejects unknown kwargs.
  - Enforces runtime type checking via `check_type(...)` using generated `annotations`.
  - Normalizes special values into Click-compatible forms (notably `JSON` via `json.dumps`, and `config_value` tuples by converting `ConfigValue`/dict to JSON strings).
  - Builds a normalized structure: `{"args": {...}, "options": {...}, "defaults": defaults}` keyed by CLI option/arg names (without leading dashes).
  - Ensures required params are present.
  This is the core mapping/validation layer between Python calls and CLI invocation.

- `_lazy_load_command(cli_collection, flow_parameters, _self, name)` — Implements lazy subcommand binding:
  - Resolves `flow_parameters` if passed as a string method name (used with `"_compute_flow_parameters"`).
  - Calls `cli_collection.get_command(None, name)` and wraps it with `extract_group(...)` or `extract_command(...)`.
  - Caches the resulting callable on `_self` via `setattr`.
  Critical for CLI command discovery without eagerly traversing everything, and important for correctness when flow context/config affects available parameters.

- `get_annotation(param: click.Parameter) -> TTuple[Type, bool]` — Converts a Click parameter into a Python typing annotation and a `is_vararg` flag:
  - Handles `nargs == -1` (varargs), `required`, `multiple`, and tuple-like nargs.
  This is key to generating usable stubs/signatures for the programmatic API.

- `get_inspect_param_obj(p, kind)` — Builds an `inspect.Parameter` for a Click `Argument` or `Option` plus the corresponding annotation:
  - If vararg, creates a `VAR_POSITIONAL` parameter named `"args"`.
  - Otherwise uses `p.name`, `p.default`, and the derived annotation.
  Central to producing accurate Python-callable method signatures.

- `loaded_modules` — Module-level cache keyed by `flow_file` to avoid re-importing flow modules repeatedly during API creation.

- `extract_flow_class_from_file(flow_file: str) -> FlowSpec` — Loads a Python file as a module and finds exactly one `FlowSpec` subclass:
  - Temporarily prepends the flow’s directory to `sys.path`.
  - Uses `importlib.util.spec_from_file_location` + `exec_module`.
  - Supports `CustomFlowDecorator` by unwrapping via `kls._flow_cls`.
  - Errors if zero or multiple FlowSpec classes found.
  This enables `MetaflowAPI.from_cli(...)` to compute parameters and wrap commands in the correct `flow_context`.

- `class MetaflowAPI(object)` — Base class for all dynamically generated API “nodes” (root, groups, commands):
  - `__init__(self, parent=None, flow_cls=None, config_input=None, **kwargs)` — Stores parent chain, initial command kwargs under `self._API_NAME`, flow class/config input, and a cache for computed parameters.
  - `parent` (property) — Returns `_parent` (or `None`).
  - `chain` (property) — Returns `_chain`, the internal structured representation of invoked commands and parameters.
  - `name` (property) — Returns `_API_NAME` (used as the command name in chain construction).
  - `from_cli(cls, flow_file: str, cli_collection: Callable) -> Callable` — Main entry point to create a programmatic API factory for a given flow file and Click CLI collection:
    - Calls `extract_flow_class_from_file`.
    - Wraps CLI with `config_options_with_config_input(...)` and `add_decorator_options(...)` inside `flow_context`.
    - Creates a dynamic subclass via `type(...)` with:
      - `_API_NAME = flow_file`
      - `_internal_getattr` wired to `_lazy_load_command(..., "_compute_flow_parameters")`
      - `__getattr__` wrapper that re-enters `flow_context` before resolving lazy commands
    - Extracts top-level params via `extract_all_params(cli_collection)` and returns a callable `_method` whose signature/annotations/defaults mirror Click’s parameters and whose body returns the root API object with validated params.
    This is the heart of “expose CLI as Runner methods” behavior.
  - `execute(self) -> List[str]` — Flattens the chain into CLI argv tokens:
    - Walks parents to build the full chain.
    - Appends command names, positional args, and `--option` pairs.
    - Special-cases boolean flags using sentinel `"flag"` to omit values.
    This is where the chain becomes an actual command list suitable for subprocess execution.
  - `_compute_flow_parameters(self)` — Computes flow-level `Parameter` objects (used to inject `--<param>` options into certain commands):
    - Only valid on the root “start” API object.
    - If `CLICK_API_PROCESS_CONFIG` is enabled, “fakes” enough Click processing to resolve config inputs (`config`/`config-value`) including `datastore`/`quiet`, and calls `self._config_input.process_configs(...)`.
    - Calls `self._flow_cls._process_config_decorators(...)` which may return a new flow class, then lists parameters via `self._flow_cls._get_parameters()` excluding `IS_CONFIG_PARAMETER`.
    - Caches results in `_cached_computed_parameters`.
    This is crucial for correct command signatures/option injection when configs can add/modify flow parameters.

- `extract_all_params(cmd_obj: Union[click.Command, click.Group])` — Collects Click params and produces:
  - `params_sigs`: ordered `inspect.Parameter` objects for method signature
  - `arg_parameters` / `opt_parameters`: ordered click params for later validation/mapping
  - `annotations`: typing annotations for each param name
  - `defaults`: default values
  Used by `from_cli()`, `extract_group()`, and `extract_command()` to generate “stub-like” methods.

- `extract_group(cmd_obj: click.Group, flow_parameters: List[Parameter]) -> Callable` — Converts a Click group into a callable method that returns a new dynamic `MetaflowAPI` subclass instance representing that group:

  - Generates a callable `_method` with signature/annotations/defaults; `_method` validates kwargs and returns the group API object with `parent=_self`.

  - Generates a callable `_method` with signature/annotations/defaults; `_method` validates kwargs, appends `{cmd_obj.name: method_params}` to `_self._chain`, then returns `_self.execute()`.
  This is where individual CLI commands become final “runner-ready” argv lists.

- **Stub/signature generation:** `get_annotation()`, `get_inspect_param_obj()`, and `extract_all_params()` generate `inspect.Signature`, `__annotations__`, and defaults—this is effectively the “stub generation” mechanism the agent expects (runtime, not static files).

output.pr_body: When injecting commands to the runner, the stubs generated were not accurate or complete. This addresses this issue
output.masca_optimized:
### MASCA (optimized for this PR: Runner API command injection + stub/signature correctness)

#### **M — Modify / Goal**
Fix how CLI commands are surfaced through the **Runner API** when commands are dynamically injected. Specifically, ensure the dynamically generated callable methods (the “stubs”: docstrings, names, signatures, annotations, defaults) accurately and completely represent the underlying Click CLI commands/options.

#### **A — Affected Components (where the behavior is implemented)**
1. **Runner command injection layer**
   - `metaflow/runner/metaflow_runner.py`
     - `RunnerMeta.__new__` dynamically injects methods onto `Runner` based on `get_runner_cli()`.
     - The wrapper `_injected_method(...)` determines how the injected method looks to Python (name, signature, docstring).

2. **Click→Python API/stub generation layer**
   - `metaflow/runner/click_api.py`
     - Creates Python callables from Click groups/commands with generated `inspect.Signature` and `__annotations__`.
     - Key conversion points: `extract_all_params`, `get_annotation`, `get_inspect_param_obj`.
     - Command/group method creation: `extract_group`, `extract_command`.
     - Validation/normalization (impacts completeness of accepted kwargs and correct defaults): `_method_sanity_check`.

3. **CLI root definition (command tree source-of-truth)**
   - `metaflow/cli.py`
     - Defines the Click command hierarchy (`start` command collection, lazy-loaded subcommands).
     - Runner/click_api must introspect the right root command and handle lazy groups correctly.

#### **S — Specific Suspected Failure Modes (what makes stubs inaccurate/incomplete)**
- Injected Runner methods likely have a generic `(*args, **kwargs)` signature (from `_injected_method`) instead of the underlying Click-derived signature.
- Docstrings and method names may be copied, but signature/annotations/defaults may not be preserved during injection.
- Lazy-loaded click subcommands/groups may not be traversed or resolved at stub-generation time.
- Flow-parameter injection (commands with `has_flow_params`) may not be reflected properly in the generated signature.

#### **C — Concrete Change Strategy**
- Ensure the injected Runner methods preserve (or forward) the Click-API-generated callable’s:
  - `__signature__` (critical for stubs/introspection)
  - `__annotations__`
  - `__doc__`
  - `__name__` / `__qualname__` (optional but helps tooling)
- If incomplete parameters stem from click_api extraction:
  - Adjust `extract_all_params` / `get_inspect_param_obj` / `get_annotation` so generated callables match Click semantics (options vs args, required/defaulted, `multiple`, varargs).
  - Ensure `extract_command` reflects `has_flow_params` injection consistently in stub/signature.

#### **A — Acceptance Criteria (how we know it’s fixed)**
- `dir(Runner(...))` shows all expected injected command methods.
- `inspect.signature(Runner(...).<command>)` matches the Click command’s options/args as exposed by `MetaflowAPI.from_cli(...).<command>`.
- Type hints and defaults are present where available.
- Docstrings are accurate and complete.

---

### Primary Code Hotspots
- Injection wrapper: `RunnerMeta.__new__` (likely needs to propagate signature metadata from the wrapped function)
- Stub generation: `extract_all_params`, `get_inspect_param_obj`, `get_annotation`, `extract_command`, `extract_group`

Implements Click-to-Python method generation (signatures/annotations/defaults/docs). If stubs are incomplete or inaccurate, extraction/annotation/signature synthesis here is a prime source.
Defines the Click command hierarchy; needed to ensure the runner click API introspects the correct command tree root and handles lazy subcommands, which can affect command completeness.

functions_to_modify.reason: This is where methods are injected onto Runner. The wrapper likely needs to preserve the wrapped runner_subcommand’s __signature__/__annotations__/__doc__ so stubs reflect the real command API.
Creates the root dynamic API object and top-level callable with extracted params. If root-level stubs are incomplete, the extraction and binding here is involved.
Central routine producing inspect.Parameters, annotations, defaults from Click objects; inaccuracies in stubs commonly originate here (missing params, wrong kinds, wrong defaults).
Computes Python typing for Click params (required/multiple/nargs). Incorrect typing/vararg handling leads to inaccurate stubs.
Builds inspect.Parameter objects and determines parameter kinds (positional, keyword-only, varargs). This directly impacts method signatures that tooling interprets as stubs.
Wraps a Click command into a Python callable; also injects flow params when cmd_obj.has_flow_params. This injection must be reflected in the generated signature/doc for completeness.
Wraps Click groups into Python callables and installs subcommands. Missing commands or incomplete group API can arise from how groups are extracted and subcommands attached.
Lazy loading affects which commands appear and when. If commands are missing from the Runner API, this resolver/binder is a likely contributor.

output.analysis_summary: The Runner API exposes CLI commands by dynamically injecting methods onto Runner (metaclass RunnerMeta) and by generating Python-callable command wrappers from Click command objects (click_api.py). The PR description points to inaccurate/incomplete generated “stubs” when injecting commands; in this codebase, “stubs” correspond to runtime-introspection metadata: inspect.Signature, annotations, defaults, and docstrings produced by click_api and potentially lost during RunnerMeta wrapping. The key modification targets are RunnerMeta.__new__ (to preserve wrapped callable metadata) and the click_api extraction/synthesis functions (extract_all_params/get_annotation/get_inspect_param_obj/extract_command/extract_group/_lazy_load_command) which determine what parameters and commands exist in the first place. The CLI definition in metaflow/cli.py is relevant as the source command tree and its lazy-loading behavior can impact completeness.

- `steps`: Ordered list of steps, each with:
  - `operation`: What to do (e.g., "Add validation method")
  - `file_to_modify`: Relative path of the file to modify (e.g., "src/module.py")
  - `function_to_modify`: Name of the function to modify (e.g., "ClassName.method_name")
  - `reason`: Technical motivation
  - `side_effects`: Potential impacts

## IMPORTANT RULE for function_to_modify
- If the file to modify is a Python file (.py), specify the function/method to modify
- If the file is NOT a Python file (e.g., .js, .html, .css, .json, .yaml, .md, etc.),

- Each step must be atomic and implementable
- The order of steps must be logical (dependencies first)
- For Python files: always specify the function in the format "FunctionName" or "ClassName.method_name"
- For non-Python files: function_to_modify must be null
- The side_effects should mention tests, dependents, and API impacts
- You MUST generate a number of step EQUAL to the number of the python functions you are intendeed to modify nomore neither less

## Step Examples

### For Python files:
```json
{
  "operation": "Add time_spanned_alpha method",
  "file_to_modify": "manimlib/animation/animation.py",
  "function_to_modify": "Animation.time_spanned_alpha",

  "function_to_modify": null,
  "reason": "Add new route for the authentication endpoint",
  "side_effects": "Requires server restart to apply changes"
}
```

- Docstrings and method names may be copied, but signature/annotations/defaults may not be preserved during injection.
- Lazy-loaded click subcommands/groups may not be traversed or resolved at stub-generation time.
- Flow-parameter injection (commands with `has_flow_params`) may not be reflected properly in the generated signature.

#### **C — Concrete Change Strategy**
- Ensure the injected Runner methods preserve (or forward) the Click-API-generated callable’s:
  - `__signature__` (critical for stubs/introspection)
  - `__annotations__`
  - `__doc__`
  - `__name__` / `__qualname__` (optional but helps tooling)
- If incomplete parameters stem from click_api extraction:
  - Adjust `extract_all_params` / `get_inspect_param_obj` / `get_annotation` so generated callables match Click semantics (options vs args, required/defaulted, `multiple`, varargs).
  - Ensure `extract_command` reflects `has_flow_params` injection consistently in stub/signature.

#### **A — Acceptance Criteria (how we know it’s fixed)**
- `dir(Runner(...))` shows all expected injected command methods.
- `inspect.signature(Runner(...).<command>)` matches the Click command’s options/args as exposed by `MetaflowAPI.from_cli(...).<command>`.
- Type hints and defaults are present where available.
- Docstrings are accurate and complete.

## Analysis Summary
The Runner API exposes CLI commands by dynamically injecting methods onto Runner (metaclass RunnerMeta) and by generating Python-callable command wrappers from Click command objects (click_api.py). The PR description points to inaccurate/incomplete generated “stubs” when injecting commands; in this codebase, “stubs” correspond to runtime-introspection metadata: inspect.Signature, annotations, defaults, and docstrings produced by click_api and potentially lost during RunnerMeta wrapping. The key modification targets are RunnerMeta.__new__ (to preserve wrapped callable metadata) and the click_api extraction/synthesis functions (extract_all_params/get_annotation/get_inspect_param_obj/extract_command/extract_group/_lazy_load_command) which determine what parameters and commands exist in the first place. The CLI definition in metaflow/cli.py is relevant as the source command tree and its lazy-loading behavior can impact completeness.

steps.operation: Preserve Click-derived callable metadata (signature, annotations, docstring, name) when injecting Runner methods so IDEs/introspection see accurate stubs instead of a generic wrapper
steps.reason: RunnerMeta currently wraps/attaches dynamically generated Click API callables onto Runner; if the wrapper function is what gets attached without copying `__signature__`, `__annotations__`, `__doc__`, and naming attributes, the injected methods will appear inaccurate/incomplete to `inspect.signature`, type checkers, and help() output.
steps.side_effects: May change how `help(Runner.<cmd>)`, `inspect.signature`, and autogenerated docs behave; ensure the wrapper still forwards args/kwargs correctly and does not break runtime invocation. Tests that assert presence/shape of Runner API methods may need updates.

steps.operation: Ensure the root Runner-facing API object is built from the correct Click root and that the returned top-level callable/object retains the extracted signature/annotations (including when lazy subcommands exist)
steps.reason: If the CLI root/group is misidentified or the root callable’s metadata is not propagated, downstream command extraction can be incomplete and the resulting Runner-injected callables may start from an already-wrong or partially-loaded command tree.
steps.side_effects: Could change which commands are discoverable immediately vs lazily; may affect any code that depends on current (possibly incomplete) enumeration of commands from the Runner/MetaflowAPI objects.

steps.operation: Fix parameter collection to fully and deterministically include all Click params (options/arguments), including injected flow params, and produce a stable ordering compatible with Python signatures
steps.reason: Inaccurate/incomplete stubs often originate from missing params, wrong inclusion rules, or inconsistent ordering when translating Click’s parameter model to Python `inspect.Parameter` lists. This function is the central aggregator used by command/group extraction.
steps.side_effects: Signature shapes may change (new params appear, ordering changes, defaults corrected). Downstream wrapper generation and any callers passing kwargs may need to align with the corrected parameter names/requirements.

steps.operation: Correct typing/annotation inference for Click parameters (required vs optional, multiple/nargs, flag/value options) so generated `__annotations__` reflect actual CLI semantics
steps.reason: Even if parameters are present, wrong annotations (e.g., missing Optional, incorrect list/tuple types for `multiple`/`nargs`) make stubs misleading for IDE autocomplete and type checking, and can hide completeness issues.
steps.side_effects: Type hints exposed to users will change; may require updating any documentation or tests that assert specific annotations. Should not change runtime behavior, but may impact static tooling expectations.

steps.operation: Generate accurate `inspect.Parameter` objects (kind, default, varargs/kwargs behavior, keyword-only) from Click params, matching how users are expected to call Runner methods
steps.reason: Incorrect `Parameter.kind` (positional-only vs keyword-only), defaults, or handling of variadic parameters leads directly to wrong `inspect.signature` output, which is the core “stub correctness” issue described in the PR.
steps.side_effects: May alter call conventions suggested by IDEs; if callers relied on positional passing where it should be keyword-only (or vice versa), they may need to adjust. Ensure compatibility with existing Runner invocation patterns where possible.

steps.operation: Ensure extracted command wrappers expose complete metadata and include flow-parameter injection in the generated signature/docstring consistently
steps.reason: This is where a Click command becomes a Python callable. If flow params are injected at runtime but not reflected in the created `__signature__` / `__annotations__` / `__doc__`, the wrapper will be incomplete even if parameter extraction is otherwise correct.
steps.side_effects: Command callables may gain additional parameters in their signature; documentation/help output may expand. Any code that introspects available kwargs may change accordingly.

steps.operation: Ensure group extraction correctly attaches all subcommands (including those behind lazy loaders) and that group-level callables preserve correct metadata
steps.reason: Missing commands in the Runner API can come from group traversal/attachment logic. Groups must enumerate/register subcommands in a way that supports both discovery (dir/attribute access) and correct per-subcommand callable metadata.
steps.side_effects: `dir()` / attribute listing on group API objects may change (more commands visible). If previously-hidden lazy commands become visible earlier, it may trigger additional imports or initialization during introspection.

steps.operation: Fix lazy command resolution so that when a lazy command is accessed, it is fully materialized with correct signature/annotations/docstring and then cached/registered on the API object for future introspection
steps.reason: If lazy-loaded commands are only partially loaded or not re-registered with full metadata, stubs will be incomplete and commands may appear missing until invoked. Proper lazy loading should preserve parity between “discovered” and “callable” APIs.
steps.side_effects: May change timing of imports or command construction during attribute access/introspection. Needs care to avoid performance regressions and to prevent repeated loading or inconsistent caching across accesses.

step_plan.summary: Fixes Runner API command injection so dynamically attached command methods accurately mirror the underlying Click CLI commands. Ensures injected methods and Click-derived wrappers preserve complete signatures, annotations, defaults, docstrings, and lazy-loaded command availability for correct introspection and tooling support.
